function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
When evaluating future orbital AI compute satellites, which disclosed accounting policies or asset categories provide the closest precedent for treatment? Does the filing explicitly resolve whether such assets would be capitalized or expensed?

steps.reasoning_content:
The user is asking about accounting policies in the SpaceX S-1 filing related to orbital AI compute satellites. I need to look at the filing to understand:
1. What accounting policies are disclosed for similar assets (satellites, space assets, etc.)
2. Whether the filing explicitly resolves whether such assets would be capitalized or expensed

Let me start by parsing the SpaceX S-1 filing and then retrieving relevant information about accounting policies for satellites and similar assets.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

arguments.prompt: What accounting policies does the SpaceX S-1 disclose for satellites, space assets, orbital assets, property plant and equipment, intangible assets, and capitalization vs expensing? Include any discussion of useful lives, depreciation, amortization, and asset categories. {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
Based on the provided S-1 filing chunks, SpaceX discloses detailed accounting policies for its property, plant, and equipment (PP&E), satellites, space assets, and intangible assets. These policies reflect the company's hybrid aerospace, connectivity, and AI infrastructure operations. 

Here is a summary of the disclosed accounting policies:

### 1. Property, Plant, and Equipment (PP&E) & Space Assets
*   **Valuation:** PP&E is stated at cost less accumulated depreciation.
*   **Asset Categories and Estimated Useful Lives:** The company provides the following schedule for its PP&E:
    *   **Servers and networking equipment:** 5 - 6 years
    *   **Satellites:** 3 - 5 years
    *   **Machinery and equipment:** 3 - 10 years
    *   **Flight vehicle hardware:** 5 - 25 flights (depreciated based on expected number of average flights)
    *   **Data center infrastructure:** 20 - 25 years
    *   **Launch sites:** 7 - 20 years
    *   **Buildings and improvements:** 30 years
    *   **Leasehold improvements:** Shorter of 7 - 20 years or the life of the lease
*   **Flight Vehicle Classification:** 
    *   **Reusable components:** Boosters (first stages), fairings, Merlin engines, and Dragon capsules are classified as PP&E.
    *   **Expendable components:** Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy second stages are not reusable and are recorded as inventory until launched.
    *   **Starship:** Currently in the development stage. A majority of Starship costs are expensed to Research and Development as incurred.

### 2. Satellites and Orbital Assets
*   **Capitalized Costs:** Satellites include costs to build (parts, labor, and allocated overhead) as well as capitalized launch costs incurred by the Space segment to launch the satellites to orbit (which includes an allocation of the flight vehicle costs).
*   **Useful Lives:** Broadband satellites are estimated to have a **5-year useful life**, while the first-generation mobile satellites are estimated to have a **3-year useful life**.
*   **Useful Life Estimation Criteria:** The company estimates useful lives based on engineering studies, historical on-orbit performance, propellant life, utilization patterns, design enhancements across generations, planned transitions to newer satellite technology, orbit-raise timing, expected service capability, and the evolution of constellation density and technology.
*   **Depreciation:** Satellites are depreciated using the straight-line method over their estimated useful lives. If the average useful life of satellite assets changed by just one year, it would have an approximately $480 million impact on operating income for the year ended December 31, 2025.

### 3. Depreciation and Amortization Policies
*   **Primary Method:** Assets are depreciated primarily using the **straight-line method** over their estimated useful lives.
*   **Flight Vehicles Exception:** Flight vehicles and spacecraft are depreciated over the **expected number of average flights** rather than a time-based life. Estimating average flights requires management to consider recovery and refurbishment success rates, refurbishment economics, customer acceptance limits for reflown hardware, expected future mission manifests, and anticipated retirement timing (e.g., transitioning from Falcon to Starship).
*   **Leasehold Improvements:** Depreciated over the shorter of their estimated useful lives or the related lease term.
*   **Revision of Estimates:** Management periodically reviews useful life estimates with engineering and operations teams. When useful lives or expected remaining flights are found to be shorter or longer than originally estimated, the company adjusts the rate of depreciation prospectively.

### 4. Capitalization vs. Expensing Policies
*   **Maintenance and Repairs:** Expenditures that do not extend the lives of the respective assets are **expensed as incurred**.
*   **Refurbishment and Enhancements:** Significant refurbishment, renewals, and enhancements that increase the functionality, output, or expected life of an asset are **capitalized** and depreciated ratably over the identified useful life.
*   **Capitalized Interest:** The company capitalizes certain interest costs associated with the significant acquisition or construction of long-term assets (e.g., AI infrastructure data centers, launch facilities). 
    *   Capitalization begins once activities necessary to get the asset ready for its intended use commence.
    *   It is calculated using the average accumulated expenditures and the company's weighted average borrowing rate.
    *   Capitalization ceases on portions substantially completed and ready for their intended use.
*   **Share-Based Compensation:** Share-based compensation can be capitalized into PP&E.
*   **Routine Disposals:** Routine asset disposals, scrapping, and gateway decommissions are charged to Cost of Revenue or SG&A depending on the nature of the asset.

### 5. Impairment Evaluation
*   **Evaluation Level:** Impairment is evaluated at the lowest level for which identifiable cash flows are largely independent of the cash flows of other assets and liabilities.
*   **Triggering Events:** The company reviews PP&E for impairment whenever events or circumstances indicate the carrying value may not be recoverable (e.g., significant changes in asset use, technological developments reducing utility, declines in forecasted cash flows, negative industry trends).
*   **Measurement:** If estimated future cash flows are less than the carrying value, an impairment charge is recognized to the extent the carrying value exceeds its estimated fair value.
*   **Income Statement Classification:** Routine disposals and recurring operational losses go to Cost of Revenue or SG&A. Impairments considered outside the normal course of business are recorded as explicit impairment charges. (For context, the company noted a $24 million impairment in Q1 2025 for damaged flight vehicles in the Space segment, and no material impairments in Q1 2026).

### 6. Intangible Assets
*   The filing notes the existence of **finite-lived intangible assets** and **indefinite-lived intangible assets and goodwill**, the valuation of which requires significant judgment and estimates. However, the specific amortization schedules and categories for these intangibles are truncated in the provided text chunks.

steps.reasoning_content: I have a good understanding of the accounting policies now. Let me also check if there's any specific discussion of AI compute satellites or future orbital AI assets in the filing.

arguments.prompt: Does the SpaceX S-1 filing discuss orbital AI compute satellites, AI infrastructure in space, future satellite generations, or any forward-looking statements about AI compute assets in orbit? Also, does the filing explicitly state whether future satellite assets would be capitalized or expensed? {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
Based on the provided text from the SpaceX S-1 filing, here are the answers to your questions:

**1. Does the filing discuss orbital AI compute satellites, AI infrastructure in space, future satellite generations, or forward-looking statements about AI compute assets in orbit?**

Yes, the filing extensively discusses all of these topics, primarily within the preamble and prospectus summary:

*   **Orbital AI Compute Satellites & AI Infrastructure in Space:** The filing frequently mentions "orbital AI compute infrastructure," "orbital data centers," and "AI compute satellites." It specifically notes plans for "massive AI compute satellite constellations—with potentially millions of satellites" and "modular orbital AI compute shells." 
*   **Future Satellite Generations:** The document references "next-generation Starlink satellites" and "next-generation, high-performance AI compute satellites built for high volume, low cost, and with the reliability required for long-duration operation in space." It also mentions that "early satellites generating 100 kilowatts of compute power" will scale up from there.
*   **Forward-Looking Statements:** The text is filled with forward-looking projections, such as expecting to "begin deploying our orbital AI compute satellites as early as 2028" and aiming to "launch 100 gigawatts of AI compute capacity on solar-powered satellites each year." It also projects transporting "approximately one million metric tons to orbit annually" to power this compute capacity. 

**2. Does the filing explicitly state whether future satellite assets would be capitalized or expensed?**

No, the filing **does not explicitly state** the accounting treatment (whether capitalized or expensed) specifically for *future satellite assets*. 

However, it does provide related financial context regarding current and future infrastructure:
*   It notes that the company has incurred "significant capital expenditures" for the "design, development, and deployment of our satellite constellations, launch vehicles, ground stations... AI compute infrastructure, data centers, and other supporting infrastructure."
*   In the notes to the financial statements, it mentions that "Construction in progress is primarily comprised of ongoing construction and expansion of the facilities and equipment as well as AI infrastructure that has not yet been placed in service."
*   It states that "Interest is capitalized during the construction period for significant long term construction projects, such as the AI infrastructure data centers and launch facilities."
*   It mentions recording "impairment charges of $24 million related to the write off of damaged flight vehicles in the Space segment," which implies that flight vehicles are treated as capitalized assets subject to impairment. 

While this implies a capital expenditure model for these assets, the provided text does not contain an explicit accounting policy statement declaring whether the *future* orbital AI compute satellites will be capitalized or expensed once deployed.

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have a comprehensive understanding. Let me also check the Starship development policy more carefully, since orbital AI compute satellites are described as future assets that may be in development. The Starship policy is interesting because it says "A majority of Starship costs are expensed to Research and Development as incurred" - this is a key precedent for assets in development.

Let me also check if there's any specific discussion of how the company treats assets that are in development vs. those that are operational.

arguments.prompt: What does the SpaceX S-1 filing say about Starship development costs, research and development expense policy, construction in progress, and how assets in development are treated? Also, what is the policy for capitalization of internally developed software or AI infrastructure? {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
Based on the provided excerpts from the SpaceX S-1 filing, here is what the document reveals about Starship development costs, R&D policies, construction in progress, asset treatment, and AI infrastructure capitalization:

**1. Starship Development Costs & R&D Expense Policy**
*   **Composition of Costs:** Starship development costs consist of test flight hardware, Raptor engines, employee compensation (salaries, benefits, share-based compensation), tooling and equipment expenses, depreciation for R&D equipment, and allocated overhead. 
*   **Expense Policy:** Because Starship is currently in the development stage, the majority of these costs are **expensed to Research and Development as incurred**. 
*   **Scale of Investment:** SpaceX has invested over $15 billion in Starship. In 2025, the company accelerated its Starship timeline, causing Space segment R&D to surge to $3,004 million (up from $1,835 million in 2024). This increase was driven by $779 million in higher production costs, $218 million in higher launch costs, and $185 million in higher engineering costs.
*   **Future Expectations:** R&D is expected to continue increasing in absolute dollars and as a percentage of revenue in 2026 as the company invests in commercialization.

**2. Treatment of Assets in Development (Post-Commercialization)**
*   **Shift to Capitalization:** Once Starship reaches commercialization (defined as delivering payload to orbit), the accounting treatment will shift. Starship costs generally **will be capitalized** rather than expensed as R&D.
*   **Depreciation:** Once capitalized, these costs will be depreciated. The depreciation will be recorded in the cost of revenue of the segment associated with the payload delivered. 
*   **Flight Vehicle Depreciation:** For reusable flight vehicles (like Falcon 9 boosters, fairings, Merlin engines, and Dragon capsules), depreciation is computed based on the expected number of average flights for each vehicle rather than straight-line.
*   **Inter-segment Launches:** For launches of SpaceX's own Starlink satellites, no inter-segment revenue is recognized. Instead, those launch costs are capitalized in satellites within Property, plant, and equipment, net.

**3. Construction in Progress (CIP)**
*   **Definition:** CIP is primarily comprised of ongoing construction and expansion of facilities, equipment, and AI infrastructure that has not yet been placed in service.
*   **Capitalized Interest:** Interest is capitalized during the construction period for significant long-term construction projects, such as AI infrastructure data centers. For the year ended December 31, 2025, SpaceX capitalized $169 million of interest, which is included in CIP.
*   **Impairments:** The company records impairment charges for damaged assets or abandoned production lines. For example, in 2025 and 2024, SpaceX recorded $38 million and $63 million in impairment charges, respectively, related to a damaged flight vehicle in the Space segment and abandoned/damaged hardware in the Connectivity segment.

**4. Capitalization of Internally Developed Software or AI Infrastructure**
*   *(Note: The provided text does not detail a specific policy for internally developed software, but it extensively outlines the capitalization of AI infrastructure).*
*   **AI Capital Expenditures:** SpaceX is making massive capital expenditures for AI infrastructure, which include costs related to obtaining third-party GPUs, manufacturing their own GPUs, and constructing, leasing, maintaining, and expanding data centers.
*   **Surging CapEx:** AI capital expenditures for 2025 increased by $7,094 million to $12,727 million, compared to $5,633 million in 2024 and just $463 million in 2023. This is driven by the rapid expansion of terrestrial data centers (like the COLOSSUS clusters) and supporting infrastructure.
*   **Accounting Treatment:** These AI data centers and infrastructure are treated as Property, plant, and equipment. They are stated at cost, less accumulated depreciation, and depreciated using the straight-line method over their estimated useful lives once placed in service. 
*   **Strategic Flexibility:** The company retains the flexibility to adjust spending across segments. If near-term data center needs decrease or ramp slower than expected, SpaceX may reduce future AI capital expenditures and reallocate those funds to other segments.

steps.message: I now have sufficient information to provide a comprehensive answer.
steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the accounting policies. Let me synthesize this into a final answer.

Key findings:

1. **Closest precedents for orbital AI compute satellites:**
   - **Satellites (existing Starlink satellites)**: Capitalized as PP&E, depreciated straight-line over 3-5 years (5 years for broadband, 3 years for first-gen mobile). Costs include build costs (parts, labor, overhead) and capitalized launch costs.
   - **Servers and networking equipment**: 5-6 years useful life
   - **Data center infrastructure**: 20-25 years useful life
   - **AI infrastructure**: Treated as PP&E, capitalized, depreciated straight-line
   - **Construction in Progress (CIP)**: For AI infrastructure not yet placed in service
   - **Starship (in development)**: Majority of costs expensed to R&D as incurred; will be capitalized once commercialized (delivering payload to orbit)

2. **Does the filing explicitly resolve whether such assets would be capitalized or expensed?**
   - No, the filing does NOT explicitly state the accounting treatment for future orbital AI compute satellites specifically.
   - However, the filing provides strong precedents:
     - Existing satellites are capitalized as PP&E
     - AI infrastructure (terrestrial) is capitalized as PP&E
     - Assets in development (like Starship) are expensed to R&D until commercialization, then capitalized
     - Construction in progress is capitalized for AI infrastructure not yet placed in service

3. **Forward-looking statements about orbital AI compute satellites:**
   - Plans to begin deploying orbital AI compute satellites as early as 2028
   - Aiming to launch 100 gigawatts of AI compute capacity on solar-powered satellites each year
   - Plans for "massive AI compute satellite constellations—with potentially millions of satellites"
   - "Modular orbital AI compute shells"
   - "Next-generation, high-performance AI compute satellites"

So the answer is:
- The closest precedents are: (1) existing satellite accounting policy (capitalized as PP&E, depreciated straight-line over 3-5 years), (2) AI infrastructure/data center policy (capitalized as PP&E), and (3) Starship development policy (expensed to R&D until commercialization, then capitalized).
- The filing does NOT explicitly resolve whether future orbital AI compute satellites would be capitalized or expensed. The orbital AI compute satellites are described as forward-looking plans (deployment as early as 2028), and the filing does not provide a specific accounting policy for them.
- However, by inference from existing policies, the most likely treatment would be:
  - During development: expensed to R&D (similar to Starship)
  - Once deployed/operational: capitalized as PP&E (similar to existing satellites and AI infrastructure)
  - Useful life: likely 3-5 years (similar to existing satellites) or potentially longer given the "long-duration operation in space" language

Let me submit the final answer.

arguments.final_result:
## Closest Accounting Precedents for Future Orbital AI Compute Satellites

The SpaceX S-1 (filed May 20, 2026) describes "orbital AI compute satellites," "orbital data centers," and "modular orbital AI compute shells" as **forward-looking plans** — with deployment targeted "as early as 2028" and ambitions to scale to "100 gigawatts of AI compute capacity on solar-powered satellites each year." Because these assets do not yet exist, the filing does not contain a dedicated accounting policy for them. However, three disclosed asset categories and policies provide the closest precedents:

### 1. Existing Satellites (Starlink broadband and mobile) — closest operational precedent
- **Classification:** Property, plant, and equipment (PP&E), stated at cost less accumulated depreciation.
- **Capitalized costs:** Build costs (parts, labor, allocated overhead) **plus capitalized launch costs** (including an allocation of flight vehicle costs) incurred by the Space segment to place satellites in orbit.
- **Useful lives:** 5 years for broadband satellites; 3 years for first-generation mobile satellites.
- **Depreciation method:** Straight-line over estimated useful lives.
- **Useful-life estimation criteria:** Engineering studies, historical on-orbit performance, propellant life, utilization patterns, design enhancements across generations, planned transitions to newer technology, orbit-raise timing, expected service capability, and evolution of constellation density/technology.
- **Sensitivity:** A one-year change in average satellite useful life would have moved 2025 operating income by approximately $480 million.

### 2. AI Infrastructure / Data Centers — closest terrestrial AI precedent
- **Classification:** PP&E (servers, networking equipment, data center infrastructure).
- **Useful lives:** Servers and networking equipment 5–6 years; data center infrastructure 20–25 years.
- **Capitalized interest:** Interest is capitalized during construction for significant long-term projects such as AI infrastructure data centers ($169 million capitalized in 2025).
- **Construction in Progress (CIP):** CIP "is primarily comprised of ongoing construction and expansion of the facilities and equipment as well as AI infrastructure that has not yet been placed in service."
- **CapEx scale:** AI capital expenditures reached $12,727 million in 2025 (up from $5,633 million in 2024 and $463 million in 2023).

### 3. Starship (in-development flight vehicle) — closest "asset-in-development" precedent
- **Current treatment:** Because Starship is in the development stage, the **majority of costs are expensed to Research and Development as incurred** (test flight hardware, Raptor engines, compensation, tooling, depreciation of R&D equipment, allocated overhead).
- **Post-commercialization treatment:** Once Starship reaches commercialization (defined as delivering payload to orbit), costs generally **will be capitalized** rather than expensed as R&D, and depreciated into the cost of revenue of the segment associated with the payload delivered.
- **Reusable vs. expendable distinction:** Reusable components (boosters, fairings, Merlin engines, Dragon capsules) are PP&E; expendable Falcon 9/Falcon Heavy second stages are inventory until launched.

### Supporting capitalization vs. expensing framework
- **Maintenance and repairs:** Expensed as incurred.
- **Refurbishment/enhancements that extend life or functionality:** Capitalized and depreciated ratably.
- **Impairment:** Evaluated at the lowest level with largely independent cash flows; triggering events include technological developments reducing utility, declines in forecasted cash flows, and negative industry trends.

---

## Does the Filing Explicitly Resolve Capitalize vs. Expense for Orbital AI Compute Satellites?

**No.** The filing does **not** explicitly state whether future orbital AI compute satellites would be capitalized or expensed. The orbital AI compute satellite program is described entirely in forward-looking language (deployment "as early as 2028," scaling to "100 gigawatts" annually, "potentially millions of satellites"), and no accounting policy is provided for assets that do not yet exist.

### Reasonable filing-supported inference
Although not explicitly resolved, the disclosed policies strongly imply a bifurcated treatment by analogy:

| Phase | Closest precedent | Implied treatment |
|---|---|---|
| **Pre-deployment / development** (design, prototyping, test flights) | Starship development-stage policy | Expense to R&D as incurred |
| **Construction / pre-service** (built but not yet placed in service) | CIP for AI infrastructure | Capitalize as CIP; capitalize related interest |
| **Operational / on-orbit** (deployed and generating service) | Existing satellite policy + AI infrastructure policy | Capitalize as PP&E (including build costs and capitalized launch costs); depreciate straight-line over estimated useful life |

The most likely useful-life analog would be the existing satellite range (3–5 years), though the filing's emphasis on "long-duration operation in space" for next-generation AI compute satellites could support a longer life — a determination that, per the disclosed policy, would depend on engineering studies, on-orbit performance, propellant life, and planned technology transitions. Given the disclosed sensitivity ($480 million operating-income impact per one-year change in average satellite useful life), this estimate would be highly material.

### Disclosure discipline note
The orbital AI compute satellite program is a **roadmap target / commercialization expectation**, not a historical outcome. Any accounting treatment for these assets is therefore an inference from existing policies, not an explicit disclosure. The filing does not specify:
- A useful life for AI compute satellites
- Whether AI compute payloads would be depreciated separately from the satellite bus
- Whether inter-segment launch costs would be capitalized into the satellites (as is done for Starlink today)
- Whether any portion of compute-related R&D would continue to be expensed post-deployment

---

## Conclusion

The closest disclosed precedents are (1) the existing satellite accounting policy (capitalized as PP&E, depreciated straight-line over 3–5 years, including capitalized launch costs), (2) the AI infrastructure / data center policy (capitalized as PP&E with capitalized interest during construction), and (3) the Starship in-development policy (expensed to R&D until commercialization, then capitalized). The filing **does not explicitly resolve** whether future orbital AI compute satellites would be capitalized or expensed; by inference from these precedents, development-stage costs would likely be expensed to R&D and post-deployment operational assets would likely be capitalized as PP&E — but this is a reasonable inference, not an explicit disclosure.

results.content:
## Closest Accounting Precedents for Future Orbital AI Compute Satellites

The SpaceX S-1 (filed May 20, 2026) describes "orbital AI compute satellites," "orbital data centers," and "modular orbital AI compute shells" as **forward-looking plans** — with deployment targeted "as early as 2028" and ambitions to scale to "100 gigawatts of AI compute capacity on solar-powered satellites each year." Because these assets do not yet exist, the filing does not contain a dedicated accounting policy for them. However, three disclosed asset categories and policies provide the closest precedents:

### 1. Existing Satellites (Starlink broadband and mobile) — closest operational precedent
- **Classification:** Property, plant, and equipment (PP&E), stated at cost less accumulated depreciation.
- **Capitalized costs:** Build costs (parts, labor, allocated overhead) **plus capitalized launch costs** (including an allocation of flight vehicle costs) incurred by the Space segment to place satellites in orbit.
- **Useful lives:** 5 years for broadband satellites; 3 years for first-generation mobile satellites.
- **Depreciation method:** Straight-line over estimated useful lives.
- **Useful-life estimation criteria:** Engineering studies, historical on-orbit performance, propellant life, utilization patterns, design enhancements across generations, planned transitions to newer technology, orbit-raise timing, expected service capability, and evolution of constellation density/technology.
- **Sensitivity:** A one-year change in average satellite useful life would have moved 2025 operating income by approximately $480 million.

### 2. AI Infrastructure / Data Centers — closest terrestrial AI precedent
- **Classification:** PP&E (servers, networking equipment, data center infrastructure).
- **Useful lives:** Servers and networking equipment 5–6 years; data center infrastructure 20–25 years.
- **Capitalized interest:** Interest is capitalized during construction for significant long-term projects such as AI infrastructure data centers ($169 million capitalized in 2025).
- **Construction in Progress (CIP):** CIP "is primarily comprised of ongoing construction and expansion of the facilities and equipment as well as AI infrastructure that has not yet been placed in service."
- **CapEx scale:** AI capital expenditures reached $12,727 million in 2025 (up from $5,633 million in 2024 and $463 million in 2023).

### 3. Starship (in-development flight vehicle) — closest "asset-in-development" precedent
- **Current treatment:** Because Starship is in the development stage, the **majority of costs are expensed to Research and Development as incurred** (test flight hardware, Raptor engines, compensation, tooling, depreciation of R&D equipment, allocated overhead).
- **Post-commercialization treatment:** Once Starship reaches commercialization (defined as delivering payload to orbit), costs generally **will be capitalized** rather than expensed as R&D, and depreciated into the cost of revenue of the segment associated with the payload delivered.
- **Reusable vs. expendable distinction:** Reusable components (boosters, fairings, Merlin engines, Dragon capsules) are PP&E; expendable Falcon 9/Falcon Heavy second stages are inventory until launched.

### Supporting capitalization vs. expensing framework
- **Maintenance and repairs:** Expensed as incurred.
- **Refurbishment/enhancements that extend life or functionality:** Capitalized and depreciated ratably.
- **Impairment:** Evaluated at the lowest level with largely independent cash flows; triggering events include technological developments reducing utility, declines in forecasted cash flows, and negative industry trends.

---

## Does the Filing Explicitly Resolve Capitalize vs. Expense for Orbital AI Compute Satellites?

**No.** The filing does **not** explicitly state whether future orbital AI compute satellites would be capitalized or expensed. The orbital AI compute satellite program is described entirely in forward-looking language (deployment "as early as 2028," scaling to "100 gigawatts" annually, "potentially millions of satellites"), and no accounting policy is provided for assets that do not yet exist.

### Reasonable filing-supported inference
Although not explicitly resolved, the disclosed policies strongly imply a bifurcated treatment by analogy:

| Phase | Closest precedent | Implied treatment |
|---|---|---|
| **Pre-deployment / development** (design, prototyping, test flights) | Starship development-stage policy | Expense to R&D as incurred |
| **Construction / pre-service** (built but not yet placed in service) | CIP for AI infrastructure | Capitalize as CIP; capitalize related interest |
| **Operational / on-orbit** (deployed and generating service) | Existing satellite policy + AI infrastructure policy | Capitalize as PP&E (including build costs and capitalized launch costs); depreciate straight-line over estimated useful life |

The most likely useful-life analog would be the existing satellite range (3–5 years), though the filing's emphasis on "long-duration operation in space" for next-generation AI compute satellites could support a longer life — a determination that, per the disclosed policy, would depend on engineering studies, on-orbit performance, propellant life, and planned technology transitions. Given the disclosed sensitivity ($480 million operating-income impact per one-year change in average satellite useful life), this estimate would be highly material.

### Disclosure discipline note
The orbital AI compute satellite program is a **roadmap target / commercialization expectation**, not a historical outcome. Any accounting treatment for these assets is therefore an inference from existing policies, not an explicit disclosure. The filing does not specify:
- A useful life for AI compute satellites
- Whether AI compute payloads would be depreciated separately from the satellite bus
- Whether inter-segment launch costs would be capitalized into the satellites (as is done for Starlink today)
- Whether any portion of compute-related R&D would continue to be expensed post-deployment

---

## Conclusion

The closest disclosed precedents are (1) the existing satellite accounting policy (capitalized as PP&E, depreciated straight-line over 3–5 years, including capitalized launch costs), (2) the AI infrastructure / data center policy (capitalized as PP&E with capitalized interest during construction), and (3) the Starship in-development policy (expensed to R&D until commercialization, then capitalized). The filing **does not explicitly resolve** whether future orbital AI compute satellites would be capitalized or expensed; by inference from these precedents, development-stage costs would likely be expensed to R&D and post-deployment operational assets would likely be capitalized as PP&E — but this is a reasonable inference, not an explicit disclosure.
